demoTitle: Spotting Digital Waste in Code gives you energy

steps.content: Before we start, introduce yourself and then interview me
I am Sparky, your AI co-host. Curious, concise, and professional. I will ask Wilco three quick questions so everyone knows who is speaking and why this topic matters today.
Go ahead
First question for Wilco: who are you, and what do you spend your time working on?
Next question
Second question for Wilco: what do you actually do, and why do software, architecture, and sustainability belong in the same conversation for you?
One more

Final question for Wilco: where does that perspective come from? Part of the answer is upbringing. Growing up around heavy industry made energy use, material flows, and waste feel visible and real from the start.

media.caption: A glimpse of the environment that shaped how I think about energy, industry, and waste.

steps.content: What is this session about?
Spotting digital waste in code gives you energy because once you see waste clearly, you can do something about it.
What will I learn here?
You move through four stages: awareness, dialogue, decision frames, and action. The goal is to make waste visible and discussable.
Where does this start?
It starts with a visual cue. Before code, you are invited to notice the act of looking closely.
Who is Freek

**This is Freek.**

Freek loves wildlife spotting.

When Freek spots a rare bird, he does not just go outside. He records it at https://waarneming.nl/.

That helps researchers and policymakers understand what is living out there.

What you notice today can make a difference for nature conservation tomorrow.

**Be like Freek.**
What does spotting teach you?
Spotting trains your eyes. You go out early, keep looking, and slowly learn to recognize what deserves attention.
What do you notice first?
A plastic bottle. A paper bag. Waste that was there all along, but now stands out immediately.
Why didn't I see it before?
Habit makes waste disappear into the background.
What else makes it easy to miss?
Another reason is ownership. If it feels like someone else's problem, it becomes easy to ignore.
When can you not ignore it anymore?
It reaches the point where ignoring it is no longer possible.
When does it become a value question?
The shift happens when value becomes visible. Waste looks very different once you ask what something is worth.
What changes once you frame it as value?
The tree is framed as real value, roughly 150,000 to 250,000 euros. That reframes careless loss as measurable damage.
How does that relate to software?
You bring in Electricity Maps to make infrastructure consumption feel physical. Compute is not abstract; it runs somewhere real, with location, intensity, and trade-offs.

media.caption: Electricity Maps view with the live generation mix.

steps.content: So what's the core idea?
Here the core definition lands: digital waste is cost without value. That cost can be money, energy, capacity, or attention.
Can you show that in the cloud?
This becomes concrete with AWS. Waste is not abstract here either. It shows up in the infrastructure choices teams make every day.
Where do you see that choice?
It starts with instance selection. The platform gives you many shapes, but that flexibility also makes overprovisioning easy.
Any caveat before we compare?
In this story, the terms virtual machines, containers, and pods are treated as interchangeable. The point is the waste pattern, not the infrastructure taxonomy.
What happens at low utilization?
At 25% utilization, a shared 16-vCPU AWS instance uses about 340 kWh per year, while a dedicated on-prem 16-core server uses about 1,600 kWh per year.
Why is there such a difference between AWS and on-prem?

Because the comparison is not one dedicated machine versus another dedicated machine. In AWS, that workload runs on shared infrastructure, so the energy burden of the physical server is spread across many tenants. On-prem, a lightly used dedicated server still carries most of its baseline power cost by itself.
What about higher utilization?
At 75% utilization, a shared 16-vCPU AWS instance uses about 640 kWh per year, while a dedicated on-prem 16-core server is around 2,700 kWh per year.
How do you quantify that?
Then you quantify it. Consolidating three 25% VMs into one 75% VM can save roughly two full instance costs, or about 13,000 euros per year with m5.4xlarge on-demand pricing.
What should I look for in a real system?
Look for three signals: too much capacity with too little use, slowness without full CPU, or one saturated part while the rest waits.
What forms does digital waste usually take?
You meet it as idle resources, structural overcapacity, repeated default settings, or hidden compute that adds no visible value.
Why does this stay around for years?
Because technical debt makes change expensive. Tight coupling, maintenance burden, and fear of breakage keep waste in place.
How do I think beyond electricity savings?
The question changes. Not only 'how do we save power?' but 'is this workload healthy, efficient, and worth running at all?'
Can you show that visually?
This visual makes the pivot concrete by separating power use from utilization. A system can consume plenty without delivering much.
What does that reinforce?
It reinforces the same point: healthy workloads balance value, efficiency, and operational quality, not just raw activity.
Can you request core utilization for a virtual machine in Linux?

Yes. In Linux, `/proc/stat` exposes cumulative CPU time counters for the whole system and for each core. The image shows those `cpu`, `cpu0`, `cpu1`, and similar lines. Each line reports time spent in modes like user, system, idle, and iowait. By reading the counters twice and comparing the change over time, you can estimate utilization per core, even inside a virtual machine.

media.caption: Example of `/proc/stat` CPU counters in Linux.

steps.content: Can a tiny code change really matter that much?
Only 40 lines of code can hide a massive gap in latency, work done, and energy consumed.
What example do you use?
It starts with a concrete OpenJDK issue. That makes the case specific immediately, not abstract.
Why does that catch attention?
Because it makes the idea feel real. You can immediately see that even a tiny code change can have a surprisingly large effect on performance, work done, and energy use.
What stands out in that story?
A tiny commit in a long stream of work suddenly matters. That is exactly the point: small code can hide very large waste.
How do you introduce the concrete case?
A bug report and a tiny implementation detail become the entry point. That makes the waste feel concrete, not theoretical.
What's the core technical idea?
The core idea is simple: reading procfs means opening text and parsing it, while direct clock access is simpler and faster.
What does the old implementation look like?
Here is the old procfs-based path. The important thing is not the line count, but the amount of work hidden behind it.

code.caption: Opening the procfs stat file and reading its contents.
Skipping the command string, parsing fields, and returning user time.

steps.content: Where do the first hotspots show up?
The first hotspot is opening the file. Even before parsing, path resolution and setup already cost real work.
What about the read path?
Then the read and close path show up. That is where kernel work, copying, and teardown add more cost than the tiny code suggests.
Where does parsing show up?
Parsing is another hotspot. The ASCII conversion work is branch-heavy and surprisingly expensive for such a small-looking path.
Can you model that at a higher level?
Yes. A simple model links frequency, micro-operations, voltage, and switching. It is not perfect physics. It is a way to reason about where dynamic work is happening.
Can you show that on real code?
Yes. Here the code is shown with inline annotations for estimated micro-operations and energy cost. The point is to make hidden runtime work visible directly next to the code itself.

code.caption: Annotated example with inline estimates taken from the screenshot.

steps.content: Could GenAI predict the bottlenecks from that code?
That leads to the real question: given this annotated code and the theoretical model, could GenAI point to the likely hotspots before profiling confirms them?
What hotspots would it probably point to first?

A plausible answer would be: `fopen()`, `fread()`, and `sscanf()`. Each one concentrates a different kind of work. Opening the procfs file drives path resolution and kernel setup. Reading it moves data from kernel space to user space. Parsing it turns ASCII text into numbers with branch-heavy conversion logic.

code.caption: 1. `fread()` block
code.content:
statlen = fread(stat, 1, 2047, fp);   // ~0.00010 mWh, ~300k uops (sys_read + copy + kernel formatting)
stat[statlen] = '\0';                // ~0.0000001 mWh, ~5 uops (store)
fclose(fp);                           // ~0.00002 mWh, ~80k uops (sys_close + teardown)

code.caption: 2. `fopen()` block
code.content:
os::snprintf_checked(proc_name, 64, "/proc/self/task/%d/stat", tid); // ~0.000002 mWh, ~5k uops (formatting)
fp = os::fopen(proc_name, "r");                                   // ~0.00003 mWh, ~100k uops (open + VFS walk)
if (fp == nullptr) return -1;                                      // ~0.0000001 mWh, ~3 uops

code.caption: 3. `sscanf()` block
code.content:
count = sscanf(s, "%c %d %d %d %d %d %lu %lu %lu %lu %lu %lu %lu", // ~0.00002 mWh, ~60k uops (parsing + conversions)
  &cdummy, &idummy, &idummy, &idummy, &idummy, &idummy,
  &ldummy, &ldummy, &ldummy, &ldummy, &ldummy, &user_time, &sys_time);
if (count != 13) return -1;                                        // ~0.0000001 mWh, ~5 uops

steps.content: So what changes in the better path?
The better path asks the kernel directly for the right clock. Less parsing, less copying, fewer syscalls, and much better latency.

code.caption: The optimized path.

steps.content: Can you show that visually too?
Yes. This visual shows the shift from the heavier procfs path to the more direct clock-based path.
How should I use this code case in the talk?
Check it out later, not now. The code case should sharpen your eye, not pull attention away from your own systems.
How do I turn this into backlog items?
Map each waste item to yearly cost, energy or CO2, performance impact, and risk. That is how it becomes backlog-worthy.
How do I phrase that as an outcome?
Do not pitch implementation details. Frame it as an outcome, like reducing infrastructure cost by consolidating underused workloads.
What actions come first?
Start with safe quick wins. Not a platform revolution, but cleanup, consolidation, and right-sizing.
How do teams make room for that work?
Give it room to happen: reserve sprint capacity or a quarterly target. Otherwise urgent delivery will always win.
Who should own it?
No owner means no living backlog item. Add labels, ownership, and a reason to revisit the work.
What happens if teams leave it alone?
If you do nothing, inefficiency keeps running: more cost, more energy use, and more systems slowly gathering dust.
What decision framework should I leave with?
Use three frames: value, cost, and exception. Ask who uses it, what it costs, and whether the trade-off is intentional.
What choices does that lead to?
Then choose: remove it, combine it, calibrate it, or consciously accept it with a named owner.
How should I start after this talk?
Start with one application. Ask what you already do, what blocks progress, and which system has the biggest user, compute, or cost impact.
What should I walk away with?
You can now spot digital waste, discuss workload quality, inspect code differently, and turn that insight into practical action.
